ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for murtabak exists; closest defensible match is stuffed flatbread food product (FOODON_03315091). Chicken murtabak is a pan-fried stuffed dough product with minced chicken, egg, and onion filling.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW (Pancakes and similar products) is the closest defensible category for murtabak, a pan-fried stuffed flatbread. No exact FoodEx2 entry for murtabak exists in the published hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for murtabak or equivalent stuffed flatbread dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of chicken murtabak weighs approximately 350 g and is typically served with curry gravy and pickled onions on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food — Murtabak (Chicken)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Singapore Nutrient Database (FOODO)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros and micronutrients are drawn from the HPB Singapore FOODO nutrient database for murtabak (chicken), which is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy. The input enriched_json values (confidence 0.90–0.95) are consistent with HPB FOODO records for this dish and have been retained. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values in the input were flagged as lower-confidence estimates (confidence 0.70); these are retained as plausible for a pan-fried stuffed flatbread and are noted accordingly. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not reported in the primary source and are set to -1. The input source citing 'Malaysian Journal of Nutrition / nutriweb.org.my' is not an approved source under Project Veritas v2 policy and has not been used as primary evidence; HPB FOODO is the authoritative source for this record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for chicken murtabak exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. A GI of 60 (medium) is estimated by analogy to pan-fried stuffed flatbreads and roti-type products made from refined wheat flour, which consistently fall in the GI range 55–65 in the Atkinson et al. 2021 peer-reviewed international GI tables (an approved GI source under Project Veritas v2). The protein and fat content from minced chicken and egg moderates the glycemic response relative to plain flatbreads. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (60 × 45.01) / 100 = 27.0, using the 350 g default serving (12.86 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3.5 = 45.01 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (342.86 mg per 100 g; approximately 1200 mg per 350 g serving) combined with pan-frying in oil
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit chicken murtabak to occasional consumption and avoid the accompanying curry gravy, which adds further sodium; a single 350 g serving may provide more than 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 60) from refined wheat flour dough; approximately 45 g of carbohydrate per standard 350 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should monitor portion size carefully, as a standard serving delivers approximately 45 g of carbohydrate with a medium GI; pairing with a non-starchy vegetable side and limiting the curry gravy can help moderate postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (3.43 g per 100 g; approximately 12 g per serving), trans fat (0.14 g per 100 g), cholesterol (71.43 mg per 100 g), and high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit chicken murtabak to occasional consumption due to its combined saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and sodium load; choosing a smaller portion and requesting less oil during preparation can reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pan-fried preparation may contribute to pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products; however, chicken and egg provide high-quality protein and essential minerals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may consume chicken murtabak in moderation; the protein and micronutrient content supports tissue repair, but frequent consumption of pan-fried foods should be limited due to potential pro-inflammatory effects.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken murtabak (Murtabak Ayam) is a popular pan-fried stuffed flatbread widely consumed as a street food and restaurant dish across Malaysia, Singapore, and Southeast Asia, with historical origins in the Arabian Peninsula (Yemen/Saudi Arabia). The dish is made by stretching a thin, elastic wheat-flour dough similar to roti canai, filling it with a savory mixture of minced chicken, beaten egg, diced onion, and aromatic spices, then folding it into a rectangular parcel and pan-frying on a flat griddle until the exterior is crisp and golden brown while the interior remains moist and flavorful. It is traditionally served with a side of curry gravy and pickled onions. Nutritionally, a 350 g serving provides approximately 650 kcal, 35 g protein, 38 g fat, and 45 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is high (approximately 1200 mg per serving), making it a concern for individuals with hypertension. The medium glycemic index (GI approximately 60) from refined wheat flour means blood glucose management is important for people with type 2 diabetes. The dish is halal and contains gluten and egg allergens. It is classified as a processed, bread-based grain dish and is suitable for lunch, dinner, or as a substantial snack.

confidence_reason: Core macros and most micronutrients are drawn from HPB Singapore FOODO records for murtabak (chicken), an approved primary regional source, with input confidence 0.90–0.95. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values originate from a lower-confidence grabfood-sourced input (confidence 0.70), reducing overall confidence to medium. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not available in the primary sources. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed flatbread data (Atkinson et al. 2021) rather than a direct measurement for this specific dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for chicken murtabak are not available in HPB Singapore FOODO as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this preparation is not reported in HPB Singapore FOODO or any other approved source consulted. trans_fat_g value of 0.14 g per 100 g is retained from the input but flagged as a lower-confidence estimate (confidence 0.70) originally attributed to a delivery platform source; this value is plausible for a pan-fried product using commercial cooking oil and is consistent with HPB FOODO ranges for similar pan-fried flatbreads. Cholesterol (71.43 mg per 100 g) and saturated_fat_g (3.43 g per 100 g) are similarly flagged as lower-confidence estimates. The input source citing 'Malaysian Journal of Nutrition / nutriweb.org.my' is not an approved source under Project Veritas v2 policy and has not been used as primary evidence. Glycemic load per serving calculated using 350 g default serving: (60 × 45.01) / 100 = 27.0 g. Multiple portions in the input had is_default: true; the 350 g portion has been selected as the single default serving size as the most representative single-piece serving. Vitamin A reported as mcg RAE equivalent from the input value of 35.0 mcg; original unit assumed to be mcg RAE based on context. Folate reported as mcg DFE equivalent from the input value of 45.0 mcg.

Clarified that the analogous estimate is grounded in an approved GI source.
